title: The Suicideï؟½s Grave
content:
آ آ آ آ  This is the scene of a manï؟½s despair, and a soulï؟½s release
آ آ آ آ  From the difficult traits of the flesh; so, it seeking peace,
آ آ آ آ  A shot rang out in the night; deathï؟½s doors were wide;
آ آ آ آ  And you stood alone, a stranger, and saw inside.

آ آ آ آ  Coward flesh, brave soul, which was it?آ آ آ آ One feared the world,
آ آ آ آ  The pity of men, or their scorn; yet carelessly hurled
آ آ آ آ  All on the balance of Chance for a state unknown;
آ آ آ آ  Fled the laughter of men for the anger of God-alone.

آ آ آ آ  Perhaps when the hot blood streamed on the daisied sod,
آ آ آ آ  Poor soul, you were likened to Cain, and you fled from God;
آ آ آ آ  Men say you fought hard for your life, when the deed was done;
آ آ آ آ  But your body would rise no more ï؟½neath this worldï؟½s sun.

آ آ آ آ  Iï؟½d choose-should I do the act-such a night as this,
آ آ آ آ  When the sea throws up white arms for the wild windï؟½s kiss;
آ آ آ آ  When the waves shake the shuddering shore with their foamy jaws;
آ آ آ آ  Tear the strand, till slipping pebbles shriek through their claws.

آ آ آ آ  The sky is loud with the storm; not a bird dare span
آ آ آ آ  From here to the mist; beasts are silent; yet for a man,
آ آ آ آ  For a soul springing naked to meet its judge, a night
آ آ آ آ  That were as a brother to this poor spiritï؟½s long flight.

آ آ آ آ  But he had chosen, they tell me, a dusk so fair
آ آ آ آ  One almost thought there were not such another-there.
آ آ آ آ  The air was full of the perfume of pines, and the sweet
آ آ آ آ  Sleepy chirp of birds, long the lush soft grass at his feet.

آ آ آ آ  They say there was dancing too in a house close by,
آ آ آ آ  That they heard the shot just thinking wild birds must die.
آ آ آ آ  They supped and laughed, went singing the long night through,
آ آ آ آ  And they danced unknowing the dance of death with you.

آ آ آ آ  What did you hear when you opened the doors of death?
آ آ آ آ  Was it the sob of a thrush, or a slow sweet breath
آ آ آ آ  Of the perfumed air that blew through the doors with you,
آ آ آ آ  That you fought so hard to regain the world you knew?

آ آ آ آ  Or was it a womanï؟½s cry that, shrieking into the gloom,
آ آ آ آ  Like a hand that closed on your soul clutching it from its doom?
آ آ آ آ  Was it a motherï؟½s call, or the touch of a babyï؟½s kiss,
آ آ آ آ  That followed your desperate soul down the black abyss?

آ آ آ آ  What did you see-as you stood on the other side-
آ آ آ آ  A strange shy soul amongst souls, did you seek to hide
آ آ آ آ  From the ghosts that were who judged you upon your way,
آ آ آ آ  Reckoned your sins against theirs for the judgment day?

آ آ آ آ  You feared the world, the pity of men or their scorn,
آ آ آ آ  The movements of fate and the sorrows for which you were born.
آ آ آ آ  Menï؟½s laughter, menï؟½s speech, their judging, what was it to this
آ آ آ آ  Where the eyes of the dead proclaim you have done amiss.

آ آ آ آ  Not peace did you gain, perhaps, nor the rest you had planned,
آ آ آ آ  ï؟½Neath the horrible countless eyes that you could not withstand?
آ آ آ آ  Or was it God looked from his throne in a momentï؟½s disdain,
آ آ آ آ  And you shrieked for a trial once more in the height of your pain?

آ آ آ آ  Perhaps-but who knows-when you struggled so hard for lifeï؟½s breath,
آ آ آ آ  You saw nothing passing the grave except silence and death,
آ آ آ آ  You lay shut in by the four clay walls of your cell,
آ آ آ آ  There the live soul locked up in the stiff dead bodyï؟½s shell.

آ آ آ آ  Dead, dead and coffinï؟½d, buried beneath the clay,
آ آ آ آ  And still the living soul caged in to wait decay,
آ آ آ آ  For ever alone in night of unlifting gloom
آ آ آ آ  There to think, and think, and think, in the silent tomb.

آ آ آ آ  Or was it in deathï؟½s cold land there was no perfume
آ آ آ آ  Of the scented flowers, or lilt of a birdï؟½s gay tune.
آ آ آ آ  No sea there, or no cool of a windï؟½s fresh breath,
آ آ آ آ  No woods, no plains, no dreams, and alas! no death?

آ آ آ آ  Was there no life there that manï؟½s brain could understand?
آ آ آ آ  No past, no future, hopes to come, in that strange land?
آ آ آ آ  No human love, no sleep, no day, no night,
آ آ آ آ  But ever eternal living in eternal light?

آ آ آ آ  Perhaps the soul thus springing to fill its grave,
آ آ آ آ  Found all the peace and happiness that it could crave;
آ آ آ آ  All it had lost alone was that poor bodyï؟½s part
آ آ آ آ  Which naught but grey corruption saw for its chart.

آ آ آ آ  Ah well! for us there ended all one manï؟½s life with this-
آ آ آ آ  A shot, a cry, a struggle, and a fainting womanï؟½s kiss;
آ آ آ آ  Lifeï؟½s blood let ï؟½mid the grasses-and all a world was lost,
آ آ آ آ  And no one may ever know how he paid the cost.

آ آ آ آ  He is lost in the crowd of the dead, in the night-time of death,
آ آ آ آ  A name on a stone left to tell that he ever drew breath.
آ آ آ آ  So desperate body die there, with your soulï؟½s long release,
آ آ آ آ  And unhappy spirit God grant you Eternityï؟½s peace!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Dora Sigerson Shorter, titled "The Suicideï؟½s Grave", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
